coredata.dc:title: Lanthanide MOFs based portable fluorescence sensing platform: Quantitative and visual detection of ciprofloxacin and Al3+
coredata.dc:creator: Meng, Shuang
Liu, Jiaxiang
Yang, Yuanyuan
Mao, Shun
Li, Zhuo
coredata.dc:description: In the current context of water environmental monitoring and pollution control, there's a crucial need for rapid and simple methods to detect multi-pollutant. We herein report an easy one-step hydrothermal synthesis method to produce Eu-based metal-organic frameworks (Eu MOFs), which was used as a fluorescent probe to detect the aquatic environmental pollutants of ciprofloxacin (CIP) and aluminum ions (Al3+). This fluorescent sensor enabled the cascade detection of CIP and Al3+ through fluorescence enhancement and ratio fluorescence response, respectively. The introduction of CIP significantly turned on the characteristic fluorescence of Eu MOFs at 595 nm and 616 nm through the “antenna effect”. Based on this, the sensor enables quantitative detection of CIP within a linear range of 0–120 μM with a LOD as low as 50.421 nM. In the presence of Al3+, the fluorescence emission of Eu MOFs-CIP was sharply turned off due to strong Al3+ coordination with CIP, while the blue fluorescence emission of CIP was remarkably enhanced. And thus allowing ratio fluorescence quantitative detection of Al3+ (LOD = 2.681 μM). The introduction of CIP and Al3+ in cascade resulted in distinct fluorescence color changes from colorless to red and eventually to blue, exhibiting pronounced fluorescence characteristics. This observable phenomenon enables the visual detection of CIP and Al3+ in both aqueous phase and paper test strips. By combining the analysis of fluorescence chromaticity with the use of a smartphone, the fluorescence color of test papers allows for simple quantitative determination, which provides a convenient and accessible approach for quantifying CIP and Al3+ in water environments.

head.ce:title: Lanthanide MOFs based portable fluorescence sensing platform: Quantitative and visual detection of ciprofloxacin and Al3+

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the current context of water environmental monitoring and pollution control, there's a crucial need for rapid and simple methods to detect multi-pollutant. We herein report an easy one-step hydrothermal synthesis method to produce Eu-based metal-organic frameworks (Eu MOFs), which was used as a fluorescent probe to detect the aquatic environmental pollutants of ciprofloxacin (CIP) and aluminum ions (Al3+). This fluorescent sensor enabled the cascade detection of CIP and Al3+ through fluorescence enhancement and ratio fluorescence response, respectively. The introduction of CIP significantly turned on the characteristic fluorescence of Eu MOFs at 595 nm and 616 nm through the “antenna effect”. Based on this, the sensor enables quantitative detection of CIP within a linear range of 0–120 μM with a LOD as low as 50.421 nM. In the presence of Al3+, the fluorescence emission of Eu MOFs-CIP was sharply turned off due to strong Al3+ coordination with CIP, while the blue fluorescence emission of CIP was remarkably enhanced. And thus allowing ratio fluorescence quantitative detection of Al3+ (LOD = 2.681 μM). The introduction of CIP and Al3+ in cascade resulted in distinct fluorescence color changes from colorless to red and eventually to blue, exhibiting pronounced fluorescence characteristics. This observable phenomenon enables the visual detection of CIP and Al3+ in both aqueous phase and paper test strips. By combining the analysis of fluorescence chromaticity with the use of a smartphone, the fluorescence color of test papers allows for simple quantitative determination, which provides a convenient and accessible approach for quantifying CIP and Al3+ in water environments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ciprofloxacin (CIP) is a commonly used fluoroquinolone antibiotic, valued for its potent antibacterial properties, excellent oral absorption, low toxicity, and affordability (Ahmad et al., 2006; Liu et al., 2019a; Yuan et al., 2021b). However, CIP exhibits a slow metabolism and degradation rate, making it less readily absorbed and susceptible to environmental persistence (Shi et al., 2019; Yue et al., 2022). Residues of CIP can pose risks to human health, leading to allergic reactions, intestinal diseases, central nervous system disorders, and joint damage (Gui et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2018a; Yuan et al., 2021a; Yuan et al., 2021b; Yuan et al., 2018). Many countries and organizations have established maximum residue limits for CIP in environment and certain foods (Liu et al., 2021; Tumini et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2021). The European Commission and the Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs of the People's Republic of China have stipulated that the maximum residue limit of CIP in fish should not exceed 100 μg/kg (Liu et al., 2021). Simultaneously, the contamination of water by metal ions constitutes a prominent issue. Aluminum (Al), being the third most abundant metal ion in the earth's crust, finds extensive use in various industrial applications (Ertl and Goessler, 2018; Liu et al., 2019b; Wang et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2022). However, there has been growing concern about the toxicity of Al3+ in last years. Its increased utilization has been implicated as a potential factor in conditions such as Parkinson's and Alzheimer's diseases, renal osteodystrophy, and even breast cancer (Armstrong et al., 1994; Hornung et al., 2008; Wang et al., 2010). The World Health Organization (WHO) has set the maximum allowable concentration of Al3+ in drinking water at 7.41 μM (Rahman et al., 2017).

Various techniques have been developed for qualitative and quantitative analysis of CIP or Al3+in last decades. These include High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) (Liu et al., 2013), Inductively Coupled Plasma-Atomic Emission Spectroscopy/Mass Spectrometry (ICP-AES/ICP-MS) (Chen et al., 2010), Spectrophotometry (Pascual-Reguera et al., 2004), Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy (AAS) (Doner and Ege, 2005), Enzyme Linked Immunoassay (ELI) (Buglak et al., 2019), and electrochemical method (Fang et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2023; Xia et al., 2019). However, these conventional analytical techniques are limited in practical applications due to their high cost, complex operation, confinement to laboratory settings, and lack of portability. Fluorescence sensing is a rapidly developing environmental detection technique in recent years, and has been widely studied in the detection of various organic small molecules due to its relative ease of operation, technical simplicity and wide adaptability (Tang et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020). Meanwhile, the fluorescence method, as an economical on-line monitoring method, can also be used for various targets, including metal ions and anions (Guo et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2022). Yin et al. (Yin et al., 2017) synthesized a novel silver nanoparticle to achieve quantitative detection of CIP by significantly enhancing the fluorescence of CIP. Kim et al. (Kim et al., 2015) designed a composite material comprising rhodamine and carbon quantum dots (CDs), achieving selective fluorescence detection of Al3+ by using the ratiometric sensing mechanism of Förste resonance energy transfer.

Among the various fluorescent materials including organic fluorescent dyes, semiconductor quantum dots, CDs, metal nanoclusters, and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), luminescent MOFs (L-MOFs) have attracted much attention in last years (An et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2023; Jie et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2022; Wu et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2020a; Zhao et al., 2022). MOFs have recently emerged as a class of promising multifunctional porous hybrid materials consisting of metal ions (clusters) and organic ligands linked by coordination (Dolgopolova et al., 2018). And the unique performance advantages, including controllable pore size, easy surface modification, high specific surface area et.al have led to the widespread application in various fields such as adsorption, catalysis, and sensing (Bi et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2023). Particularly, L-MOFs can also be designed and modulated according to the target analyte, offering the unique advantages of high tunability and functionality in sensing (Jie et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2021a). Lanthanide MOFs (Ln-MOFs) with lanthanide element ions as nodes are especially important fluorescent materials due to their unique optical properties such as wide band width, long lifetime, large Stokes shift and ligand-related luminescence sensitization (Dong et al., 2015; Sun et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2022). Meanwhile, Ln-MOFs are considered as excellent candidates for metal ions, organic molecules and anions detection systems, and they have been developed for the detection of multiple contaminants (Wu et al., 2021). For example, Sh et al. (Sh. Mohammed Ameen et al., 2022) synthesized a bi-ligand Eu-based MOF as a reference signal by a simple one-step solvothermal method, which combined with carbon dots to achieve ratiometric fluorescence detection of Ag+ and cysteine. Chen et al. (Chen et al., 2022) constructed a bi-functional fluorescent nanoprobe using LnMOFs and inorganic nanoclay as raw materials, which can specifically recognise pyridine dicarboxylic acid and Cu2+. Wu et al. (Wu et al., 2021) synthesized a stable lanthanide MOF with red fluorescence, Eu-MOFs, by a solvothermal method, which showed good selectivity and sensitivity in the fluorescence detection of Fe3+ and benzaldehyde. However, the investigation and development of dual-function fluorescent sensors with the ability to detect both CIP and Al3+ remains a knowledge gap to date.

In this study, a Ln-MOFs material, Eu MOFs, was synthesized, which serving as a fluorescent probe for the cascade detection of CIP and Al3+. Initially, Eu MOFs ws synthesized via a facile one-step hydrothermal synthesis (Scheme 1a), facilitating highly sensitive fluorescence detection of CIP. Upon the addition of CIP, the red fluorescence of Eu MOFs was significantly enhanced, showing a linear relationship with CIP concentration within a specific range. Subsequently, the addition of Al3+ disrupted the chelation interaction between Eu MOFs and CIP, resulting in the disappearance of red fluorescence and the emergence of bright blue fluorescence. Moreover, the immobilization of Eu MOFs onto filter paper enabled the construction of a visual paper-based sensing device (Scheme 1b), presenting a novel strategy for portable and on-site detection of CIP and Al3+.

Europium chloride hexahydrate (EuCl3·6H2O, >99 %), isophthalic acid (IPA, 99 %), 2-methylimidazole (MIM, 98 %), ciprofloxacin (CIP, ≥98 %), tetracycline (TC, 96 %), tobramycin (TOB, 95 %), chloramphenicol (CHL, 98 %), kanamycin sulfate (KANA, >94 %), clarithromycin (CLARY, ≥98 %), sulfadiazine (SD, 98 %), 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA, 99 %), glutamic acid (Glu, ≥99 %), glycine (Gly, 99 %), L-cysteine (L-Cys, 99 %), l-ascorbic acid (L-AA, 99.7 %), glutathione (GSH, 99 %), aluminum chloride hexahydrate (AlCl3·6H2O, 99.99 %), nickel chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O, 99 %), barium chloride hexahydrate (BaCl2·6H2O, ≥99 %), cadmium chloride hemipentahydrate (CdCl2·5/2H2O, 98 %), potassium chloride (KCl, ≥99.5 %), zinc chloride (ZnCl2, ≥98 %), sodium chloride (NaCl, ≥99.9 %), anhydrous calcium chloride (CaCl2·2H2O, ≥96 %), lead chloride (PbCl2, 99 %), magnesium chloride (MgCl2·6H2O), copper chloride (CuCl2, 98 %), tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane(tris, ≥99.8 %), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36–38 %), absolute ethyl alcohol (≥99.7 %) were purchased from Aladdin, Sinopharm and Adamas (Shanghai). Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH = 8.5) was prepared in lab. Deionized (DI) water was used to prepare all aqueous solutions.

The Eu MOFs were prepared by hydrothermal synthesis according to the reported method (Zhao et al., 2021), as shown in Scheme 1a. In details, a mixture of IPA (83 mg), EuCl3·6H2O (366 mg) and MIM (82 mg) were dissolved in DI water (10 mL) by ultrasonic for 15 min, then was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel vessel and heated at 150 °C for 72 h. The products were alternately washed twice with anhydrous ethanol and DI water. After centrifugation and purification, the white crystal powder of Eu MOFs was obtained by vacuum drying overnight at 60 °C.

The structure and morphology of Eu MOFs were characterized by a TESCAN MIRA LMS (Czech Republic) field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM), and the energy dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDS) operated by Oxford X-MAX50 at accelerating voltages of 1–30 kV. Rigaku Ultima IV (Japan) was used to record X-ray diffraction (XRD) data under room temperature (293 K). Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum were recorded from a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS20 (USA) using KBr pellets at ambient temperature. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained via a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha (USA) X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with an Al Ka X-ray source (1486.6 eV). Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90 was employed to investigate zeta potential.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of CIP and Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: Detection of CIP: the prepared Eu MOFs were dispersed in DI water to form an aqueous suspension (250 mg/L), labeled as suspension 1 . Then the suspension 1 was added to Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 8.5) and followed by adding CIP. After complete reaction at room temperature, fluorescence spectra from 550 nm to 650 nm were recorded using an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The emission intensity at 616 nm of the mixed suspension was utilized for quantitative analysis of CIP.

Detection of Al3+: prepared the fluorescence probe stock suspension, denoted as suspension 2 , by mixing Tris-HCl buffer, suspension 1 , and specific amounts of CIP at a fixed volumetric ratio. Subsequently, different concentrations of Al3+ solutions were added to suspension 2 . After the reaction completed at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra from 385 nm to 650 nm were recorded using an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The intensity ratio of the peaks between 410 and 435 nm to peaks at 616 nm (denoted as F 1/F 2) of the mixed suspension was used for quantitative analysis of Al3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the paper-based visual sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Square pieces of approximately 1 cm2 were cut from the filter papers and incubated in suspension 1 for 24 h, then removed and dried at 60 °C in an oven. The test papers loaded with Eu MOFs were then immersed in suspensions of different concentrations of CIP for 3 s and dried naturally. The fluorescent colors of the test paper were then captured using a smartphone under a 365 nm UV lamp and the RGB (R: red channel, G: green channel, B: blue channel) values were extracted for chromaticity analysis.

A batch of the Eu MOFs-loaded test papers were immersed in specific concentration of CIP for 3 s, and dried naturally. Subsequently, 20 μL of different concentrations of Al3+ was dropped onto the prepared filter paper strips. After these test papers were allowed to naturally dry, the fluorescence images were captured for further analysis.

SEM technology was used to characterize the morphology, structure, and size of the prepared Eu MOFs. The SEM images (Fig. 1a, b) show that the Eu MOFs has a rod-shaped structure with a regular surface, almost uniform size, and good dispersion with an average diameter of about 3 μm. The element chemical distribution and composition of Eu MOFs were studied using an energy spectrometer, and the resulting EDS spectrum in Fig. S1 confirms that the Eu MOFs is mainly composed of Eu, C, and O elements. SEM element mappings in Fig. 1c–e further demonstrate that Eu, O, and C are homogenously distributed in the Eu MOFs, evidencing the successful formation of the Eu MOFs. In addition, the mapping scan of N elements revealed that almost no N element was presented in Eu MOFs (Fig. S2), indicating that MIM didn't participate in the coordination during MOFs synthesis, but only acted as an auxiliary agent in the reaction.

XRD analysis (Fig. 2a) shows that the diffraction peaks of the synthesized Eu MOFs closely match the simulated diffraction peaks, demonstrating the successful synthesis of Eu MOFs crystals. The composition of Eu MOFs was further confirmed by FT-IR spectroscopy. In Fig. 2b, the strong absorption at 3382 cm−1 is attributed to the OH stretching vibratory group, indicating the presence of hydroxyl. The characteristic bands occurring at 1655–1504 cm−1 and 1468–1311 cm−1 represent symmetric and antisymmetric tensile vibrations of the COO group in the ligand, respectively (Yang et al., 2020b). The absorption peak at 534 cm−1 may be due to the tensile vibration of EuO (Guo et al., 2021) from the interaction of Eu ions with the COO group, which demonstrates the successful synthesis of Eu MOFs.

In addition, XPS characterization was conducted to investigate the surface element composition and valence state distribution of Eu MOFs (Fig. 2c, d). The XPS survey spectrum in Fig. 2c shows the presence of C 1s, O 1s, and Eu 3d, which is consistent with the results of EDS and SEM mapping analysis. The Eu 3d spectrum obtained from Eu MOFs shows the peaks located at 1164.78, 1155.68, 1143.48, and 1135.08 eV (Fig. 2d), consistent with the bonding between oxygen atoms and europium (Mercier et al., 2006; Tan et al., 2008). The valence state of Eu is predominantly Eu3+ (3d3/2 and 3d5/2) (Yi et al., 2020). These analyses further confirmed the successful synthesis of Eu MOFs via a hydrothermal method.

The fluorescence characterization of Eu MOFs was carried out, revealing a significant red fluorescence emission at 619 nm in the solid state with an optimal excitation wavelength of 398 nm. However, when Eu MOFs were dispersed in DI water at a concentration of 1 g/L, no fluorescence was observed (Fig. S3). And there was no obvious change in solid-state fluorescence when the Eu MOFs were stored for 30 days at ambient temperature and atmospheric pressure, indicating good storage stability (Fig. S4). Additionally, a pH stability test of Eu MOFs was performed, and the results demonstrated that Eu MOFs exhibited stability under pH changes of 5–11 (Fig. S5).

As shown in Fig. 3a, the fluorescence emission spectra of Eu MOFs, CIP and Eu MOFs + CIP were measured under the excitation of 365 nm. 365 nm here is the optimized excitation wavelength as shown in Fig. S6. No fluorescence emission was observed when Eu MOFs were dispersed in DI water, while CIP suspension showed obvious blue fluorescence emission. And mixing Eu MOFs with CIP showed obvious red fluorescence emission, corresponding CIE chromaticity diagram coordinates is (0.600, 0.314) (Fig. 3b). Notably, the resulting Eu MOFs mixture displayed five fluorescence characteristic peaks, which were attributed to the characteristic peaks of CIP (410–440 nm), the 5D0 → 7F1 (595 nm), 5D0 → 7F2 (616 nm), 5D0 → 7F3 (654 nm) and 5D0 → 7F4 (702 nm) (Lu et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2015), as shown in Fig. 3a. The significant decrease of CIP in characteristic emission at 432 nm indicates an energy transfer from CIP to Eu MOF, thus providing more energy for the transition of electrons within the f orbitals in Eu3+, leading to the amplification of fluorescence.

The optimization studies were conducted by taking into account of the concentration of Eu MOFs in suspension 1 , the reaction pH and temperature. The optimized concentration Eu MOFs in suspension 1 on the fluorescence response to CIP is 250 mg/L. Fig. S7 shows the fluorescence response of Eu MOFs with CIP at pH = 7–9. Notably, the strongest fluorescence enhancement effect was observed at pH = 8.5, leading to the adoption of a Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH = 8.5) as the designated reaction medium for our investigations. Subsequently, the fluorescence enhancement effect of the reaction was investigated at the temperatures ranging from 10 to 60 °C, as depicted in Fig. S8. It was observed that the influence of temperature on the reaction was relatively marginal. Consequently, the fluorescence detection test for CIP was conducted at room temperature 25 °C. In addition, the Eu MOFs shows good fluorescence response to CIP after 30 days of storage in a dry environment (Fig. S9).

Based on the above optimized experimental conditions, the quantitative detection performance of Eu MOFs for CIP was then investigated. The concentration of CIP in suspension 1 was varied from 0 to 240 μM, and the corresponding fluorescence responses are presented in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4a shows the fluorescence emission spectra in the wavelength range of 550 nm to 650 nm. Upon the addition of CIP, the suspension 1 exhibited notable fluorescence emission peaks at 595 nm and 616 nm, with a remarkable enhancement in fluorescence intensity as CIP concentration increased. Fig. 4e exhibits the photoluminescence behavior of Eu MOFs in the presence of varying concentrations of CIP under irradiation by a UV lamp with a wavelength of 365 nm. Fig. 4b shows the trends of fluorescent intensity at 616 nm of Eu MOFs varying with CIP concentration. The fluorescence intensity increases slightly as CIP contration lower than 100 μM, followed by a rapid acceleration, and ultimately reaches a plateau state at about 220 μM. This indicates that the Eu MOFs is suitable for sensing CIP in water bodies. In the concentration range of 0–120 μM, it was found that there is a linear relationship between CIP concentration and the natural logarithm of fluorescence intensity (lnF) (Fig. 4c), by giving lnF = 0.055C CIP − 0.385 (R
2 = 0.996). The corresponding limit of detection (LOD) is 50.421 nM according to the calculation of 3σ·exp(b), where σ is the standard deviation of 20 repeated blank experiments, b is the intercept of the linear fitted line (Fu et al., 2022b; Hubaux and Vos, 1970; Yi and Zhang, 2021). Compared with the literature, Eu MOFs has a superior sensing ability for CIP (Table 1 ). The corresponding coordinates in the CIE chromaticity diagram for suspension 1 with different concentrations of CIP are displayed in Fig. 4d.

To investigate the selectivity and anti-interference of Eu MOFs as a fluorescent probe for CIP detection, several common organic substances in water bodies with similar structures or composition to CIP were tested (Fig. 5 ), including TC, TOB, CHL, KANA, CLARY, SD, DPA, Glu, Gly, L-Cys, L-AA, and GSH (Fig. S10). As shown in Fig. 5A, the fluorescence responses of Eu MOFs to the introduced substances are almost negligible compared to that of CIP, and the corresponding emission spectra are shown in Fig. S11. Such a phenomenon may be attributed to the light harvesting capacity of CIP and the exclusive coordination ability due to its unique β-diketonate configuration (Yuan et al., 2021b). In addition, the anti-interference ability of Eu MOFs is presented in Fig. 5b, which shows that the fluorescence response of Eu MOFs to CIP is far higher than other co-exist interfering substances including small organic molecules and metal ions with the same concentration. The above results demonstrate that Eu MOFs owns favorable selectivity and anti-interference properties as a fluorescent probe for the detection of CIP in aqueous environment.

Based on the strong fluorescence response of Eu MOFs to CIP and the stable fluorescence emission of Eu MOFs-CIP platform (Fig. S12), the visual dual-fluorescence signal ratio sensing of the metal ion Al3+ was realized. To prevent an excessive presence of unbound CIP from interfering with the detection of Al3+, a concentration of 150 μM CIP determined in Fig. 4b was selected for configuring the Eu MOFs-CIP detection precursor in suspension 2 . The detection capability of Eu MOFs-CIP toward Al3+ with a concentration range of 0–3.5 mM is presented in Fig. 6 . In Fig. 6a, the emission spectra (385–690 nm) were recorded under the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. As the concentration of Al3+ increases, the fluorescence intensity at 616 nm gradually decreases, while the fluorescence intensity of the characteristic peak located between 410 and 435 nm progressively intensifies with a concomitant redshift. The fluorescence intensity ratios of the peaks between 410 and 435 nm and the peaks at 616 nm (F 1/F 2) against the concentration of Al3+ is plotted in Fig. 6b. Within the Al3+ concentration range of 0–2 mM, the change of F 1/F 2 remains minimal, whereas in the high concentrations ranging from 2 mM to 3.5 mM, the ratio of F 1/F 2 increases remarkably. A linear fitting analysis was then conducted over the concentration range of 0–1.5 mM (inset in Fig. 6b), by giving the fitting equation F 1/F 2 = 2.445C Al 3+ + 0.095 (R
2 = 0.993). The corresponding LOD is 2.681 μM according to 3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation of 20 repeated blank experiments, k is the slope of the fitted line (Khan et al., 2019; Gao et al., 2023; Yu et al., 2023). The LOD is lower than the maximum permissible concentration of Al3+ in drinking water according to the WHO of 7.41 μM, which holds significant practical value. Fig. 6c presents the fluorescence emitted by Eu MOFs-CIP in the CIE chromaticity diagram upon the addition of Al3+ with different concentrations. The progressive transition of fluorescence color from red to blue with the addition of Al3+ is clearly evident. This phenomenon is further supported by the optical photographs captured under UV lamp illumination (Fig. 6d).

In order to investigate the selectivity and interference resistance of the Eu MOFs-CIP platform as a fluorescence probe for Al3+ detection, several common pollutant metal cations possible coexisting in environmental waters including Ni2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, K+, Zn2+, Na+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Cu2+ were separately tested. In Fig. 7a, the response of all ten metal cations on the F 1/F 2 can be negligible, and the corresponding emission spectra are shown in Fig. S13. The results of the interference tests are presented in Fig. 7b, in which the F 1/F 2 show a very slight variation. These results demonstrate that the Eu MOFs-CIP system has excellent selectivity and interference resistance that can be used as a fluorescence probe for detecting Al3+ in water environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the fluorescent response to CIP and Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: In order to elucidate the detection mechanism, the fluorescence spectra of CIP, CIP + Eu3+, and CIP + Eu MOFs were analyzed (Fig. S14). With the inclusion of Eu3+ in the CIP environment, the β-diketonate configuration of CIP serves as a double-dentate ligand, which facilitates the formation of a distinctive six-membered ring structure in conjunction with Eu3+, thereby initiating the energy transfer to Eu3+ via the “antennae effect”. Consequently, a pronounced augmentation of the characteristic fluorescence emission of Eu3+ is observed at 616 nm (5D0 → 7F2) (Fig. S15). It is worth noting that the Eu MOFs exhibits stronger fluorescence response to CIP, compared to Eu3+ with the same Eu content (Fig. S16). This may be due to the fact that after Eu3+ is transformed into Eu MOFs, the coordination water molecular could be replaced by ligands, thereby reducing the non-radiation quenching from the high-frequency OH oscillators (Li et al., 2023). And another possible factor is the porous characteristics of the Eu MOFs material which adsorbs CIP from water (Zhao et al., 2021), achieving a concentration-enrichment effect and therefore exhibiting higher sensitivity. This indicates that Eu MOFs has a significant response to CIP and shows potential as sensing probe for CIP sensing detection.

XPS analyses were conducted for Eu MOFs before/after the CIP detection. In Fig. 8a–b, the O 1s spectrum is split into two peaks, representing the EuO bond and the oxygen‑hydrogen bond in Eu-OH, respectively (Yang et al., 2021b). Notably, for the Eu MOF + CIP ensemble, a discernible escalation in EuO content was found, ascending from 25.56 % to 37.87 %, while the proportional representation of OH bonds dwindled from 74.44 % to 62.13 %. This might be attributed to the ciprofloxacin molecule substituting hydrogen atoms on the hydroxyl group during the detection process, and forming new complexes, Eu MOFs-CIP with oxygen on the homologous functional group on the Eu MOFs (Wang et al., 2016). The C 1s spectrum in Fig. 8c-d expose the emergence of a distinct CN bond peak at 284.88 eV after the detection process. Remarkable alterations were also found in the content ratios of π-π, CO/CO, CC, and CC bond, implying the occurrence of a notable interaction between CO and π-π within CIP detection (Yang et al., 2018). Additionally, the zeta potentials of Eu MOFs and CIP were analyzed, and the results show that both the surfaces are negatively charged, thereby dispelling the possibility of the electrostatic interactions between Eu MOFs and CIP during the detection process (Fig. S17). These results demonstrate that there are coordination interactions and π-π interactions between Eu MOFs and CIP.

XRD analysis was further performed for the Eu MOFs after the detection of CIP and Al3+, and the results are shown in Fig. S18. The diffraction pattern of Eu MOFs shows little change after CIP and Al3+ detection, indicating that Eu MOFs preserved integrated structure during the detection process. Simultaneously, it also ruled out the possibility of structural collapse causing changes in fluorescence properties.

The UV absorption spectra of Eu MOFs, CIP, Eu MOFs + CIP and Eu MOFs + CIP + Al3+ were tested to further investigate the mechanism of fluorescence detection of CIP and Al3+ by Eu MOFs (Fig. 9 ). It can be seen that Eu MOFs + CIP shows two consecutive peaks in 322–334 nm which is different from the single absorption peak of CIP, indicating the interaction force between CIP and Eu MOFs (Yuan et al., 2023). The high intensity peak of CIP at 273 nm is slightly blue-shifted from 273 nm to 271 nm that implies the chelating coordination interaction occurred between Eu MOFs and CIP (Drakopoulos and Ioannou, 1997). The above phenomenon indicates that the carboxyl group of CIP ligates with Eu3+, resulting in energy transfer from CIP to Eu3+, which further proves the “antenna effect” (Wang et al., 2018b; Yang et al., 2017). In addition, after the incorporation of Al3+ into the Eu MOFs-CIP system, the broad peak in 322–334 nm was shifted to 314–327 nm, and the high intensity peak at 271 nm was slightly redshifted. It indicates that the original coordination bond between Eu MOFs and CIP was broken and a new coordination bond was formed between CIP and Al3+. As the “antenna effect” was disrupted, the red characteristic fluorescence of Eu MOFs disappeared while the blue fluorescence of CIP itself restored and enhanced.

It has been reported that the carboxylic acid groups in CIP can be combined with metal ions, such as Ca2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Pb2+and Al3+, especially with Al3+ (Sun et al., 2022; Yuan et al., 2021b; Gao et al., 2019). In this work, the addition of Al3+ destroyed the coordination between CIP and Eu MOFs, forming the Al3+-CIP complex. To confirm this speculation, the binding constant of CIP with Eu MOFs and Al3+ at the same temperature was calculated, based on the modified Benesi-Hildebrand equation (Gao et al., 2019):
(1)
F 0
/ Δ F = α + α /
K
v n
; α = 1 /
F −
F 0
where F 0 is the fluorescence intensity of CIP, ΔF is the fluorescence variation after adding Eu MOFs and Al3+ into CIP suspension, K is binding constant and n is the complex ratio (Kaczmarek, 2018). Basing on the experimental results, the binding constant K values for K CIP-Al and K CIP-Eu MOFs were calculated to be 1.38 × 109 and 6.30 × 108, respectively (Fig. S19). These values indicate that Al3+ has a higher affinity to CIP molecules than Eu MOFs. Consequently, the addition of Al3+ gradually quenched the red fluorescence emitted by Eu MOFs, which is attributed to the disruption of the “antenna effect” mechanism caused by the competitive binding of Eu MOFs and Al3+ to CIP. In addition, it was demonstrated that the binding ability of CIP to Al3+ was also superior to that of interfering metal ions Ca2+, Cd2+, Cu2+ and Pb2+ by calculating the binding constants of CIP with the interfering metal ions (Fig. S20), which also provided a theoretical basis for the anti-interference of the Eu MOFs-CIP system for the detection of Al3+.

To evaluate the reliability of Eu MOFs as a fluorescent sensor, the tap water from our lab in Tongji University and lake water from Huangpu River in Shanghai were selected as real samples, and CIP and Al3+ were detected by standard addition method. As shown in Table 2 , the recovery rate of detected CIP is between 97.519 % and 109.637 %, and that of detected Al3+ is between 89.684 % and 105.453 %. The relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 3) was at a normal level, which verified the feasibility and reliability of Eu MOFs as a fluorescent probe for detecting CIP and Al3+ in water environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Paper-based visual detection for CIP and Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: The development and application of fluorescence test paper have further expanded the portability of fluorescence detection. Research based on this has been reported and has played an important role in multiple fields (Lv et al., 2018; Qiu et al., 2017). A portable fluorescent sensing paper strip for CIP and Al3+ detection was further developed by loading Eu MOFs on the filter paper, which enabled visual detection with the naked eye and quantitative analysis through signal reading via a smartphone. Fig. S21 shows the surface morphologies of the filter paper before/after impregnated with Eu MOFs dispersion. It can be seen that before loading, the surface of the fibers on the filter paper is flat and smooth, but after impregnation, numerous rod-shaped particles adhered to the fibers, confirming successful Eu MOFs loading onto the filter paper.

The detection results of the test paper for CIP and Al3+ are presented in Fig. 10 . As illustrated in Fig. 10a, the color of the test paper exhibits distinct changes under UV irradiation as the concentration of CIP increased, transitioning from a deep purple hue to a bright red shade. Fig. 10b demonstrates the excellent selectivity of the Eu MOFs-based test paper for CIP detection. The linear fitting of the R-values of the test paper color against the CIP concentration is shown in Fig. 10c, by giving a detection range of 300–550 μM. When different concentrations of Al3+ were added to the test paper, the fluorescence color of the paper gradually changes from red to purplish-red and eventually turns blue (Fig. 10d). Fig. 10e demonstrates the good selectivity of the test paper for Al3+ with various metal ions. The relationship between natural logarithm of the ratio of B-value to R-value (Ln(B/R)) with the concentration of Al3+ was analyzed (Fig. 10f), showing a good linear correlation, and the calculated LOD was 301.050 μM.

In summary, Eu MOFs with rod-like structure were synthesized by a simple one-pot hydrothermal synthesis method, which was first time used as a fluorescent probe for the rapid, specific, and visualized analysis of CIP and Al3+ in water environments. This innovative application not only provides an effective analytical tool but also brings new possibilities to the field of environmental monitoring. Ciprofloxacin was found specifically enhance the red fluorescence of Eu MOFs through the “antenna effect”. While Al3+ can disrupt the “antenna effect” between Eu MOFs and CIP, resulting in the quenching of red fluorescence and the restoration and enhancement of CIP intrinsic blue fluorescence due to the strong binding affinity between Al3+ and CIP. This dynamic response demonstrates the successful application of Eu MOFs in the cascaded recognition and detection of CIP and Al3+. The prepared Eu MOFs exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity for the detection of CIP, with a low LOD of 50.421 nM, which was lower than most studies. Moreover, the Eu MOFs-CIP system allowed for the ratio fluorescence detection of Al3+ with a LOD of 2.681 μM, which is below the WHO regulations. In addition, the fluorescence test paper based on Eu MOFs was fabricated for portable and disposable detection. The construction of this portable fluorescent sensing device not only achieves the cascade detection of ciprofloxacin and Al3+, but also holds the potential to improve the detection efficiency and reduce the cost, shows great practical significance.
